texto: He never had nothing of his own before, except the kid, and he can’t claim but half the credit, there, maybe less. T.J.’s blond like his mamma, and stubborn, too. Won’t let nobody hold him except her. Cries every time his daddy picks him up. Every time he looks in those wet blue eyes, he nearly loses it. His own son hates him. Can’t blame the kid for having an opinion.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Nadria Tucker: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “He never had nothing of his own before, except the kid, and he can’t claim but half the credit, t…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que tempo, alabama, birmingham pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de birmingham e fiction.

frase_original: He never had nothing of his own before, except the kid, and he can’t claim but half the credit, there, maybe less. T.J.’s blond like his mamma, and stubborn, too. Won’t let nobody hold him except her. Cries every time his daddy picks him up. Every time he looks in those wet blue eyes, he nearly loses it. His own son hates him. Can’t blame the kid for having an opinion.

texto: What’s the biggest problem facing teenagers today? Ourselves. We’re a generation of lazy underachievers who need to learn that hard work pays off. What’s your town known for? Cow manure! Hold for laughs... Actually Irondale is the setting of Fannie Flagg’s famous novel Fried Green Tomatoes at the Whistle Stop Café. Why’d you enter the Junior Miss Birmingham pageant? To win... to go to State... then Nationals... maybe get the hell out of Alabama.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Nadria Tucker que provoca esforço, persistência e resultados. Ao questionar “What’s the biggest problem facing teenagers today? Ourselves. We’re a generation of lazy underach…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre humor, alabama, birmingham, com foco em trabalho e birmingham.
